key_insight: Sulfur vacancies in MoS2 do not diffuse independently; instead, their motion is collective and changes with defect concentration, producing clustered and concentration-dependent transport regimes.
engineering_relevance: This concentration-sensitive vacancy diffusion helps explain memristive behavior in MoS2 devices and can guide defect engineering for more reliable electronic and memory-device performance.
method: Kinetic Monte Carlo simulations of sulfur-vacancy dynamics
Transition rates derived from machine-learning interatomic potential molecular dynamics
Cluster analysis across varying vacancy concentrations
evidence_signal: The abstract reports three transport regimes across concentration ranges, with immobile/small-cluster behavior at low concentration, a fluctuating connected network with concentration-dependent diffusion in the intermediate regime, and classical diffusion with anisotropic cluster growth at high concentration.
watch_item: The results depend on simulated vacancy rates and idealized defect concentrations, so device-scale behavior may also be influenced by temperature, strain, boundaries, and other real-material effects.
paper_title: Self-organized defect clustering and concentration-dependent vacancy diffusion in MoS$_2$
